---
title: "Why Rust for bioinformatics?"
subtitle: "Day 1, Lecture 1"
author: "Rust for bioinformaticians"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, ../theme.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    highlight-style: github
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-line-numbers: false
    incremental: false
    chalkboard: false
    menu: true
---

## About this lecture

- **Why** Rust matters in modern bioinformatics
- **Where** it lives in the toolbox already
- **What** it costs
- **What** this course will and will not teach

::: notes
Welcome to day 1 of the course. This is the first of two short lectures today. Twelve lectures across the week, but most of the time you spend on the keyboard, not on me. Today I want to motivate the choice of language. If you finish the lecture not convinced Rust is interesting, the exercises will be harder.
:::

## Three numbers up front

::: {style="font-size: 1.2em;"}
- **10×–100×** — typical Rust speedup over Python on bioinformatics inner loops
- **1 file** — a Rust **binary** (the single executable file the compiler produces) has zero extra deployment dependencies
- **0** — data races possible in safe Rust (enforced at **compile time** — checked when you build the program, before it ever runs)
:::

::: notes
I want to give the punchline before I give the argument, so you know where we are going. Three numbers. Performance, deployability, safety. Each of these will get its own slide.
:::

## A representative workload — counting k-mers

```python
# Python (CPython 3.12), single-threaded
from collections import Counter

def count_kmers(seq: str, k: int) -> Counter:
    return Counter(seq[i:i + k] for i in range(len(seq) - k + 1))
```

::: notes
Here is the kind of thing every bioinformatics tool does in its inner loop somewhere: count contiguous substrings of length k from a long DNA sequence. This is the Python version. Idiomatic, four lines, clear what it does. You could write it in 30 seconds.
:::

## What Rust looks like — a 3-line taste

Before the dense version, here is the smallest possible Rust example: declare a counter, loop, print.

```rust
let mut count = 0;
for base in "ACGTACGT".chars() {
    if base == 'G' || base == 'C' {
        count += 1;
    }
}
println!("{}", count);
```

- `let mut count = 0;` — declare a mutable variable (Rust variables are read-only by default)
- `for base in ...` — same shape as Python's for loop
- `println!` — print; the `!` marks it as a macro, not a function

::: notes
This is what Rust looks like for a beginner. Three lines do the work. If you can read a Python for loop, you can read this. Notice mut: Rust makes you ask for mutability explicitly, which is one of the small habits the language pushes on you. The next slide jumps to a denser example using collections and slices; if any of it looks intimidating, remember we will spend day 1 and day 2 unpacking exactly these pieces.
:::

## Same algorithm in Rust

```rust
use std::collections::HashMap;

fn count_kmers(seq: &[u8], k: usize) -> HashMap<Vec<u8>, usize> {
    let mut counts: HashMap<Vec<u8>, usize> = HashMap::new();
    for window in seq.windows(k) {
        *counts.entry(window.to_vec()).or_insert(0) += 1;
    }
    counts
}
```

::: notes
The Rust version. Same algorithm. Slightly more types. You will write code that looks essentially like this on day 3 — this is from the day-3 kmer-counts exercise. The bytes are u8 instead of characters; the dictionary is a HashMap. Otherwise: same structure, same length.
:::

## The performance gap

On a 10 megabase genome with k = 21, single-threaded:

| Implementation | Wall-clock |
|---|---|
| Python (CPython 3.12) | ~ 44 s |
| PyPy 3.10 | ~ 7 s |
| **Rust `--release`** | **~ 0.8 s** |
| Rust + `rayon`, 8 cores | ~ 0.1 s |

::: notes
When you actually run them, on a representative dataset, this is the typical gap. CPython is what you get with an idiomatic Python script. PyPy is faster but still nowhere close. Rust with the release flag is fifty times faster than CPython. Add rayon — which you will write on day 5, with a one-line change — and you get another factor of eight from your CPU cores.

Numbers vary by hardware, by k, by how much of your hot loop is in NumPy versus pure Python. But the order of magnitude is reliable.
:::

## The performance gap — visualised

![Rust --release is ~50x CPython; rayon adds another ~8x.](images/lec1-perf-bars.svg){#fig-perf-bars fig-alt="Horizontal bar chart with a log-scale wall-clock axis from 0.1 s to 100 s. Four bars from top to bottom: CPython 3.12 at 44 seconds (grey), PyPy 3.10 at 7 seconds (grey), Rust release at 0.8 seconds (highlighted yellow with orange border), Rust plus rayon on 8 cores at 0.1 seconds (green). Tick labels at 0.1, 1, 10, 100 seconds. Footer notes speedups versus CPython of roughly 1x, 6x, 55x, 440x. Wall-clock for k-mer counting at k=21 on a 10 Mb genome, across four implementations."}

## Why this matters in bioinformatics

- Reads per sample: $10^8$
- Samples per project: $10^2$ to $10^5$
- Genome / transcriptome / pangenome sizes are growing **faster than CPU clock speeds**
- Cloud bill scales linearly with wall-clock
- Iteration speed scales **inversely** with wall-clock

::: notes
A 10× speedup is more important here than in business software because our workloads scale with biology, not with users. A 10x speedup turns an overnight job into a coffee break. That changes how you iterate. It changes which experiments you bother to run.

And the clock speed gap matters: CPU cores stopped getting faster around 2005. Per-core performance has crept up by less than 2× since then. Sequencing throughput has gone up by something like 10^4 in the same period.
:::

## The deployment story — Python

```bash
$ ./run_analysis.sh
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pysam'

$ pip install pysam
ERROR: Building wheel for pysam failed (htslib not found)

$ apt install libhts-dev
E: Unable to locate package libhts-dev

$ # ...one hour later...
```

::: notes
Different angle. Suppose you give your colleague a Python tool. It depends on pysam, which depends on htslib, which depends on the right libc, which depends on the kernel version. Anybody who has done bioinformatics on an HPC cluster knows this conversation well.

Containers help. Conda environments help. They are also one more thing the user has to set up correctly, every time, before your tool runs at all.
:::

## `crates.io` replaces a whole stack of tools

Python and R deal with the dependency mess by layering **container and environment managers** on top:

- **Docker**, **Podman** — container runtimes
- **Apptainer** (formerly Singularity) — HPC-friendly containers
- **Conda**, **mamba** — package + environment managers

Each one exists because Python and R libraries pull in C, C++, Fortran, system libraries, the right libc...

Rust binaries pull in **nothing** at run time. `cargo build --release` produces a single static executable. `cargo install some-tool` fetches the source and builds it. [`crates.io`](https://crates.io) is the index — one tool, one ecosystem.

The tool *is* the artifact. No container needed.

::: notes
Containers and Conda environments exist to paper over an ecosystem problem: Python and R packages have deep, fragile native dependencies. Rust skips the problem by linking everything statically into the binary by default. The same `cargo install` command works on a laptop, an HPC node, and a Docker image — without Docker. This single change makes it dramatically easier to ship a tool that other people can actually run.
:::

## Type safety — what Python and R skip

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Python — explodes at runtime**

```python
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

add(3, "seven")
# TypeError: unsupported operand
#   type(s) for +: 'int' and 'str'
```

**R — boilerplate to defend the function**

```r
add <- function(a, b) {
  stopifnot(is.numeric(a),
            is.numeric(b))
  a + b
}
add(3, "seven")  # still errors at runtime
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Rust — caught before the program runs**

```rust
fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {
    a + b
}

add(3, "seven");
// error[E0308]: mismatched types
//   expected `i32`, found `&str`
```

No `stopifnot`, no `assertthat::assert_that`, no defensive `if (!is.numeric(x))` blocks at the top of every function. **The type signature is the check.**
:::
:::

::: notes
This is a subtle but huge productivity win. In R, defensive argument checking is so common that there are whole packages for it (assertthat, checkmate). Every function starts with a wall of stopifnot calls, and even then the errors only fire at runtime — usually deep inside a long-running pipeline. In Rust the type signature does the work. If a function takes an i32, the compiler refuses to even build a program that tries to pass a string. The "bug" never reaches a test, never reaches production, never wakes up the on-call data scientist at 2am.
:::

## Safety — what the word means here

These are the bugs that make C programs crash unpredictably — Rust catches them before the program runs.

```rust
let mut seq = vec![0u8; 100];
let r = &seq[0];           // borrow a reference into seq
seq.push(b'A');            // try to change seq while r still points in
println!("{}", r);
```

```
error[E0502]: cannot borrow `seq` as mutable because it is also
              borrowed as immutable
  --> src/main.rs:3:5
   |
 2 | let r = &seq[0];
   |          ------ immutable borrow occurs here
 3 | seq.push(b'A');
   | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ mutable borrow occurs here
 4 | println!("{}", r);
   |                - immutable borrow later used here
```

::: notes
When I say Rust is safe, I mean the compiler catches at compile time the class of bug that crashes C and C++ programs at runtime (while the program is executing on a user's machine): dangling pointers, use-after-free, data races, buffer overruns.

This is not a runtime check. There is no garbage collector (the background process Python and Java run to free unused memory automatically). It is the compiler refusing to compile broken code. The error message you see here is the actual output for the snippet above — and notice the message tells you what is wrong, where, and why. We will have more to say about borrow checker (the part of the Rust compiler that tracks who owns each piece of memory) errors on day 2.
:::

## What a segfault actually is

When a program tries to read or write a memory address it doesn't own — say, by following a stale pointer or running off the end of an array — the operating system notices and kills the process.

- **Segfault** (segmentation fault — what happens when a program touches memory it doesn't own) is the symptom you see in C and C++ when this goes wrong
- Python and Rust are designed so you almost never see one: Python checks at runtime, Rust checks at compile time

::: notes
A segfault is the operating system slamming the door. The hardware (the memory management unit) compares every address your program touches against a map of what is allowed; an out-of-bounds access traps into the OS, and the OS sends your process a signal that, by default, terminates it. It is the single most common crash mode for C programs. One of the design goals of Rust is to make this class of bug impossible without a runtime cost — we will see how on day 2.
:::

## Safety in our context — parallel `count_kmers`

```rust
use rayon::prelude::*;

let total: HashMap<Vec<u8>, usize> = sequences
    .par_iter()                       // .iter() -> .par_iter()
    .map(|seq| count_kmers(seq, 21))
    .reduce(HashMap::new, merge);
```

::: notes
The practical payoff of safety in bioinformatics: you can parallelise your inner loop across CPU cores by changing one method call, with zero mutex acquisition and zero risk of data corruption.

Day 5 has an exercise built around exactly this. .iter() becomes .par_iter(). The compiler refuses to compile any version that would race. There is no equivalent in Python or R.
:::

## Rust in bioinformatics today

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**I/O and formats**

- `noodles` — pure-Rust HTSlib
- `rust-htslib` — htslib FFI
- `needletail` — FASTA/FASTQ

**Variant calling**

- `varlociraptor`
- `longshot`
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Single-cell**

- `alevin-fry`

**Long-read QC**

- `chopper`
- `nanoq`

**General**

- `rust-bio`
- parts of `mash`, `kraken2`
:::
:::

::: notes
These are tools you have likely used or read papers about. The ecosystem is moving fast. Most of these projects either did not exist or were tiny three years ago. Noodles in particular is becoming the default I/O library in a lot of new Rust bioinformatics code.

I should also say: a lot of well-known bioinformatics tools are still written in C++ — minimap2, samtools, bcftools — and those are not going anywhere. Rust is a complement, not a replacement.
:::

## You don't have to choose — PyO3

```rust
use pyo3::prelude::*;

#[pyfunction]
fn count_kmers(seq: &[u8], k: usize)
    -> HashMap<Vec<u8>, usize>
{
    // ...same body as the Rust version above...
}
```

```python
# In your Jupyter notebook:
import my_fast_lib
counts = my_fast_lib.count_kmers(seq, 21)
```

::: notes
A common worry: "I have a Python pipeline that works. I cannot rewrite it all."

You don't have to. PyO3 lets you write a fast inner loop in Rust and expose it to Python as if it were a regular module. The Python user does not know — and does not need to know — that the function they are calling is implemented in Rust.

This is how polars works. It is how pydantic-core works. It is how tokenizers works. PyO3 is out of scope for this course, but worth knowing it exists.
:::

## What this course teaches

| Day | Theme |
|---|---|
| 1 | Toolchain, scalars, control flow, functions |
| 2 | Ownership, borrowing, strings, slices |
| 3 | Structs, enums, iterators, recursion, error handling |
| 4 | I/O, crates, modules, plotting, zip |
| 5 | Tests, `--release`, parallelism, ecosystem |
| 6 | Yew & WebAssembly — pure-Rust web apps |

::: notes
Six days. Each day has two short lectures and a stack of hands-on exercises. By Friday you will have written, tested, and benchmarked a small toolkit of real bioinformatics code: GC content, reverse complement, k-mer counting, phylogenetic-tree traversal, sequence alignment, FASTA and FASTQ parsing with noodles, a histogram plot, a parallel computation, and a web frontend.
:::

## What we **won't** cover

- Async runtimes (one narrow exception on day 6)
- Writing macros (using them is fine)
- `unsafe` code
- Explicit lifetime annotations beyond elision
- Designing generic trait hierarchies
- C FFI, embedded, GPU programming

::: notes
Deliberate omissions. Everything on this list is a real and useful part of Rust. None of them are needed to write effective bioinformatics tools. The Rust Book is there when you decide you want to go deeper, and it is excellent.
:::

## Today specifically

By end of day 1 you will be able to:

- Install Rust, scaffold and run a `cargo` project
- Read and write Rust syntax for variables, control flow, functions
- Use `match` expressions and `panic!`
- Compute GC content, base counts, complement, Phred score, Hamming distance — five small programs, with tests

::: notes
Five exercises today, each fifteen to thirty minutes. Tests are provided; your job is to make them pass. By the end of today you have working Rust on disk and a feel for the toolchain. Tomorrow we go deeper into ownership.
:::

## Let's get coding

- **Setup**: [`intro/install.qmd`](../intro/install.qmd)
- **Reference**: [`day1/00-concepts.qmd`](00-concepts.qmd)
- **First exercise**: [`day1/01-gc-content.qmd`](01-gc-content.qmd)

::: notes
Three links to start. Open the install page first if you have not yet installed Rust and VS Code. Skim the concepts page — it is a fifteen-minute reference companion to this lecture; you do not need to memorise it. Then go to exercise 1.

I will see you in lecture 2 after lunch.
:::
